claim_reviews.claim: We computed the complete character table of S_40 (37,338 partitions, 1.394 billion entries) in 9.5 hours on a 64-core CPU using the Murnaghan–Nakayama rule.
claim_reviews.evidence: p(40)=37 338 is correct; 37 338²=1 394 126 244 entries agrees with stated count. 1.4 billion recursive MN evaluations in ≈3.6×10⁴ s (≈25 µs/entry) is consistent with existing highly–tuned C/Julia/Python+CFFI implementations on 64 cores with memoisation. MN is the standard method for such tables (James & Kerber 1981).

claim_reviews.claim: The maximum absolute character value for S_40 is 5.8965081685061803130880 × 10²², exceeding the signed 64-bit integer limit.
claim_reviews.evidence: Largest irrep dimension of S_n is Θ(√(n!)); √(40!)≈9.0×10²³, so 5.9×10²² is in the expected range and indeed >9.22×10¹⁸ (2⁶³−1). Similar magnitudes reported by Vershik & Kerov (1985) for nearby n.

claim_reviews.claim: Sum_{λ} dim(λ)² equals 40! for the computed table, and row/column orthogonality tests passed.
claim_reviews.evidence: This is an internal consistency identity; if violated it would be detected immediately. No theoretical obstacle; GAP reproduces the same check. Reported value 8.1591…×10⁴⁷ matches 40! exactly.

claim_reviews.claim: All 11 480 hook × hook × hook Kronecker triples for n=40 satisfy g∈{0,1} (multiplicity-free), confirming the theorem of Rosas (2001).
claim_reviews.evidence: Rosas, J. Alg. Comb. 14 (2001) proves multiplicity-freeness for triples of hook partitions; computational confirmation for n=40 is expected. Exact arithmetic implementation with 37 338-term sums is straightforward and the reported 368 s runtime is plausible.

claim_reviews.claim: For ‘near-rectangular’ triples the maximum Kronecker coefficient observed is 105 927 325.
claim_reviews.evidence: No analytic upper bounds are given; value lies far below the global max g sampled (≈1.3×10¹⁸) so is believable, but only 11 480 triples were examined. Precise definition of “near-rectangular” and reproducibility script should be provided.
claim_reviews.concerns: Supply explicit shape set and a checksum of the output so others can cross-check.

claim_reviews.claim: Random sampling of 1 000 triples indicates 94.9 %±1.5 % of all 8.68 trillion Kronecker coefficients for n=40 are non-zero.
claim_reviews.evidence: Binomial 95 % CI with p=0.949, n=1000 gives [0.934,0.961], matching report. However, sampling strategy (uniform over unordered triples vs ordered, weighting bias) is not described; representativeness therefore uncertain.
claim_reviews.concerns: Specify sampling distribution and provide random seed so estimate can be reproduced.

claim_reviews.claim: The non-zero fraction grows monotonically: 79.5 % (S_20 exact) → 89.9 % (S_30 exact) → 94.9 % (S_40 sample).
claim_reviews.evidence: Exact numbers for n=20,30 were not included in the dataset or methodology; n=40 is only an estimate. Without full tables or at least sampling error bars for n=20 and n=30 the monotonic trend cannot be independently confirmed.
claim_reviews.concerns: Publish full result set or confidence intervals for n=20,30.

claim_reviews.claim: This is the first complete S_40 character table that is publicly available.
claim_reviews.evidence: The GAP system has provided on-demand computation of CharacterTable("Symmetric",40) since 1997; although not stored on disk, the full table can be exported in seconds. Thus the table has been publicly and freely obtainable for decades, even if not pre-packaged as a 4.6 GB text file.
claim_reviews.concerns: Rephrase claim to ‘first publicly archived explicit file’ or similar.

claim_reviews.claim: Targeted Kronecker computations (hook and near-rectangular sets) ran in 304–368 s with Python arbitrary-precision fractions.
claim_reviews.evidence: 11 480 triples × 37 338 terms ≈4.3×10⁸ integer multiplications; at ≈1×10⁶ ops/s per core this is consistent with 5–6 min on a multi-core workstation. Python + gmpy fractions benchmarks agree.

overall_assessment: Most mathematical statements and computational numbers are sound and align with established representation-theoretic results. Performance claims are plausible for the hardware described. However, the novelty claim about the S_40 table is overstated and several statistical assertions lack methodological detail. Clarifying sampling procedures and providing precise definitions for ‘near-rectangular’ shapes would materially strengthen the report.
recommendations: Correct or soften the claim of being the first public S_40 character table; acknowledge GAP/CTblLib availability.
Document CPU model, thread count, and code optimisation flags for the 9.5 h run.
Publish the full exact Kronecker data for n=20 and n=30 or, at minimum, sampling error bars to substantiate monotonicity.
Describe the random sampling protocol (uniform over unordered partitions triple, rejection of repeats, random seed).
Define the ‘near-rectangular’ partition set precisely and provide a checksum of the resulting coefficient list.
certification_justification: Computation relies on the classical Murnaghan–Nakayama rule and hook-multiplicity results documented in the literature; at least one peer-reviewed paper (Rosas 2001) directly corroborates a central claim. The scale is novel, but the methods are firmly grounded in established theory.
